title: Fatigue, jaundice, and dark urine

chart.oneLiner: 45F presents with one week of rapidly worsening fatigue, jaundice, and dark urine.

opening: I've gone really pale and tired this week, my eyes have turned yellow, and my urine looks dark.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 45-year-old woman, pale and mildly jaundiced, tired. Cooperative.

HPI: Rapidly progressive fatigue, breathlessness, and lightheadedness over a week. Noticed yellowing of the eyes and dark (tea-colored) urine. Recent viral upper respiratory illness; started a new antibiotic recently (only if asked about recent illness/medications). NO black/bloody stools, NO heavy menses, NO known liver disease.
PMH: none significant; possible underlying autoimmune tendency. MEDS: a recently started antibiotic (if asked). SOCIAL: minimal alcohol.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: pale with scleral icterus/jaundice.
- Vitals: BP 118/72, HR 112, RR 18, 37.4°C, SpO2 98% RA.
- Abdomen: mild splenomegaly if examined; no marked tenderness.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, flow murmur. No petechiae/purpura; no focal neuro deficit.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the recent illness or new antibiotic unless asked about recent infections or medications.
- Splenomegaly is revealed only if the abdomen is examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.CBC: Hgb 7.2, MCV 102 (elevated due to reticulocytosis), WBC normal, Plt 240 (NORMAL platelets — important).
labs.Smear: Spherocytes and polychromasia; NO schistocytes (argues against microangiopathy).
labs.Hemolysis: LDH HIGH, indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin HIGH, haptoglobin LOW — a hemolytic pattern.
labs.Coombs: Direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test POSITIVE — confirms autoimmune hemolysis.
labs.BMP: Cr normal (no renal involvement to suggest HUS).

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: RBBB morphology plus left axis deviation (left anterior fascicular block).
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: RBBB morphology plus left axis deviation (left anterior fascicular block). Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Elevated right hemidiaphragm with otherwise clear lungs (incidental).
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Elevated right hemidiaphragm with otherwise clear lungs (incidental). Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this anemia (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Hemolytic anemia — autoimmune (leading,7). Microangiopathic hemolysis — TTP/HUS (CANT-MISS to exclude,5). Hereditary hemolysis (G6PD/spherocytosis) (core,3). Drug-induced hemolysis (core,3). Bleeding (core,2). Names hemolysis and the imperative to look for schistocytes/thrombocytopenia to exclude TTP/HUS (core,5).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Hemolytic anemia — autoimmune. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Microangiopathic hemolysis — TTP/HUS. Also reasonable: Hereditary hemolysis (G6PD/spherocytosis); Drug-induced hemolysis; Bleeding.

coreActions.item: Names hemolysis and the imperative to look for schistocytes/thrombocytopenia to exclude TTP/HUS

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: CBC + reticulocyte count (CANT-MISS,3). Peripheral smear — spherocytes vs schistocytes (CANT-MISS,4). Hemolysis labs — LDH, haptoglobin, indirect bilirubin (3). Direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test (CANT-MISS,3). Renal function/platelets to exclude TTP/HUS (2). PENALTY: starting steroids before excluding TTP (which needs plasma exchange, not steroids) (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; CBC; Reticulocyte count; Peripheral smear; Hemolysis labs; Coombs (DAT); BMP.

penalties.item: starting steroids before excluding TTP (which needs plasma exchange, not steroids)

contraindicatedTests: starting steroids before excluding TTP (which needs plasma exchange, not steroids)
orderSensitiveActions: starting steroids before excluding TTP (which needs plasma exchange, not steroids)

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = bifascicular block (RBBB + left anterior fascicular block). CREDIT: recognizes the RBBB pattern with marked left axis deviation (7); names it as bifascicular block (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: calling it sinus tachycardia / missing the conduction abnormality (-3).
keyFindings: RBBB pattern plus marked left axis deviation
criticalFindings: Bifascicular block (RBBB + left anterior fascicular block).
commonMistakes: reporting only the RBBB and missing the axis/fascicular component
steps.idealAnswer: Bifascicular block: RBBB plus left anterior fascicular block (left axis deviation).

criticalActions: Bifascicular block (RBBB + left anterior fascicular block).
coreActions: RBBB pattern plus marked left axis deviation

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = elevated right hemidiaphragm. CREDIT: identifies the elevated right hemidiaphragm (6); gives a brief differential (eventration, phrenic nerve palsy) (4). PENALTY: calling the study normal / missing the elevated hemidiaphragm (-2).
keyFindings: gives a brief differential (eventration, phrenic palsy)
commonMistakes: mistaking the elevated diaphragm for a basal effusion or mass
steps.idealAnswer: Elevated right hemidiaphragm; consider eventration or phrenic nerve palsy.

coreActions: gives a brief differential (eventration, phrenic palsy)

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Reticulocytosis, low haptoglobin, high LDH/indirect bilirubin, spherocytes, NO schistocytes, normal platelets, and a positive Coombs. What is your diagnosis and what did you rule out?
steps.rubric: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (warm type) (6). Cites a positive DAT with hemolysis markers; explicitly rules out TTP/HUS via the absence of schistocytes, normal platelets, and normal renal function (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia, given the positive direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test with hemolysis markers (reticulocytosis, low haptoglobin, high LDH/indirect bilirubin) and spherocytes. TTP/HUS was ruled out by the absence of schistocytes, normal platelets, and normal renal function.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH hemolytic anemia: corticosteroids first-line for warm AIHA (CANT-MISS,7); folate supplementation (3); identify/treat underlying trigger (4); transfuse least-incompatible units if life-threatening (4); hematology consult (3); escalate – rituximab/IVIG/splenectomy if refractory (2). PENALTY: withholding life-saving transfusion for crossmatch incompatibility (-3); steroids for cold agglutinin without addressing cold avoidance/cause (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: Warm AIHA: corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg) first-line; folate supplementation; treat any underlying trigger (lymphoproliferative disorder, drug, infection). For severe/refractory: rituximab, IVIG, or splenectomy. Transfuse if life-threatening (least-incompatible units, do not withhold for fear of crossmatch). Hematology consult. Avoid cold exposure if cold agglutinin type.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 45F presents with one week of rapidly worsening fatigue, jaundice, and dark urine. The diagnosis is Autoimmune hemolytic anemia, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: mild splenomegaly if examined; no marked tenderness.
Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, flow murmur. No petechiae/purpura; no focal neuro deficit.
Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, flow murmur. No petechiae/purpura; no focal neuro deficit.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Autoimmune hemolytic anemia while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Autoimmune hemolytic anemia and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
criticalInterpretationItems: Bifascicular block (RBBB + left anterior fascicular block).
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Bifascicular block (RBBB + left anterior fascicular block).
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Bifascicular block (RBBB + left anterior fascicular block).
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
